  • Tools Used

Methodology

Data Overview

The raw dataset was provided as part of the VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 1 and consists of a knowledge graph representing the music industry, delivered as a JSON file and then split into two CSV files:

File Records Description
mc1_nodes.csv 17,412 All entities in the graph — Person (11,361), Song (3,615), RecordLabel (1,217), Album (996), MusicalGroup (223)
mc1_edges.csv 37,857 All relationships — PerformerOf, ComposerOf, LyricistOf, ProducerOf, RecordedBy, DistributedBy, InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, CoverOf, DirectlySamples, LyricalReferenceTo, MemberOf

Data Wrangling

The graph-structured data (nodes + edges) needed to be reshaped into flat tables for Tableau to consume. A Python script (wrangle.py) was written using pandas to perform this transformation.

No data was deleted, modified, imputed, or fabricated. All values in the output tables originate directly from the source files.

Approach: Normalised Bridge Tables

The revised wrangling script adopts a normalised bridge table architecture rather than producing a single large flattened table per question. This approach is safer for Tableau because it avoids row duplication caused by many-to-many relationships (e.g. a song with multiple performers and multiple influences would otherwise multiply rows unpredictably).

The pipeline consists of four stages:

1. Node normalisation — All node IDs and date fields (release_date, written_date, notoriety_date) are cast to integer types. A display_name field is derived from stage_name where available, falling back to name. Boolean flags (is_work, is_person_or_group, is_oceanus_folk) are added to each node for efficient downstream filtering.

2. Edge normalisation — Source and target IDs are cast to integers, and a key column is added for multigraph disambiguation.

3. Edge enrichment — A single enriched edge table is built by joining node attributes onto both the source and target of every edge (equivalent to two SQL LEFT JOINs). All subsequent tables are derived from this one enriched table, ensuring consistency.

4. Table construction — Specialised functions derive each output table from the enriched edge table using aggregation, pivoting, and filtering.

Operations Performed

Operation Description
Filter Select rows matching criteria (e.g. genre = “Oceanus Folk”, influence-type edges, Sailor Shift’s edges)
Join / Enrich Attach node attributes (name, genre, release year) to both ends of every edge in a single enriched table — equivalent to two SQL LEFT JOINs
Pivot / Unstack Reshape role counts (PerformerOf, ComposerOf etc.) from long to wide format for the works master table
Aggregate Count occurrences such as number of performers per work, influence references per song, and collaborators per artist
Derive Compute new fields — display_name from stage name, career_span_years = last − first release year, is_oceanus_folk_artist flag, draws_from_count and influenced_others_count per work
Classify Assign directional labels to influence edges — "Sailor -> Other" vs "Other -> Sailor", and "OF -> Other" vs "Other -> OF" vs "OF -> OF"

Key Entities Identified

Through filtering, the following key entities were identified for analysis:

  • Sailor Shift (Person, id=17255) — the central figure of the investigation
  • Sailor Shift’s works — 46 works linked to Sailor Shift either directly (via her own PerformerOf, ComposerOf, or LyricistOf edges) or indirectly (via her group memberships — e.g. Ivy Echos — through MemberOf edges). The v1 script captured only 38 works by missing group membership involvement; the revised script correctly captures all 46.
  • Ivy Echos (MusicalGroup) — Sailor Shift’s primary band, found via a MemberOf edge from Sailor Shift’s node
  • Oceanus Folk catalogue — 305 works filtered by genre = “Oceanus Folk”

Output Tables

The wrangling produces 9 Tableau-ready CSV files across two tiers: four normalised base tables that can be joined in Tableau’s data source editor, and five pre-aggregated summary tables ready to use directly in worksheets.

Base Tables (join in Tableau)

works_master.csv (4,611 rows × 24 columns)

A single flat record for every Song and Album in the dataset. Contributor names (performers, composers, lyricists, producers) and label names are aggregated into semicolon-delimited strings per work, with separate numeric count columns for filtering. Influence counts (draws_from_count, influenced_others_count) are pre-joined from the influence bridge.

Key columns: id, work_name, work_type, genre, release_year, written_year, notoriety_year, notable, single, is_oceanus_folk, performers, composers, lyricists, producers, num_performers, num_composers, num_lyricists, num_producers, recorded_by_labels, distributed_by_labels, draws_from_count, influenced_others_count

contributors_bridge.csv (29,882 rows × 11 columns)

One row per contributor–work relationship, covering all PerformerOf, ComposerOf, LyricistOf, ProducerOf, RecordedBy, and DistributedBy edges. A role_group column distinguishes creative contributors from label relationships. This table is the primary source for questions about who made what.

Key columns: edge_type, role_group, source_id, source_display_name, source_type, work_id, work_name, work_genre, work_release_year

influence_bridge.csv (7,407 rows × 13 columns)

One row per influence relationship across the entire dataset, covering all five influence edge types (InStyleOf, InterpolatesFrom, CoverOf, DirectlySamples, LyricalReferenceTo). Both source and target node attributes are fully resolved, making this the primary source for genre-to-genre influence flow analysis.

Key columns: influence_type, source_id, source_display_name, source_genre, source_year, target_id, target_display_name, target_genre, target_year

memberof_bridge.csv (568 rows × 8 columns)

One row per person–group membership, with both person and group attributes fully resolved. Used to trace which artists belong to which musical groups.

Key columns: person_id, person_display_name, person_type, group_id, group_display_name, group_type

Summary Tables (use directly in Tableau)

sailor_shift_career_v2.csv (46 rows × 32 columns)

Sailor Shift’s full discography — a superset of the works master filtered to her works, with additional columns describing her specific involvement. Crucially, this table distinguishes between works where she contributed directly (her own edges) and works where she was involved via group membership (e.g. through Ivy Echos). This resolves an undercounting issue in the previous version of the script, which missed 8 group-membership works.

Additional columns over works_master: sailor_involvement_modes (direct_person_edge / via_group_membership), sailor_roles (PerformerOf / LyricistOf etc.), sailor_linked_groups (e.g. Ivy Echos), is_direct_person_work, is_group_membership_work, collaborators, num_distinct_collaborators

sailor_shift_influences_v2.csv (40 rows × 14 columns)

All influence edges involving Sailor Shift or her works, with a direction column classifying each edge as "Sailor -> Other" (32 edges — she influenced others) or "Other -> Sailor" (8 edges — others influenced her). Both source and target node attributes are fully resolved, supporting timeline and genre breakdown visualisations.

Key columns: direction, influence_type, source_display_name, source_genre, source_year, target_display_name, target_genre, target_year

oceanus_folk_spread_v2.csv (601 rows × 15 columns)

All influence edges touching at least one Oceanus Folk work, with a direction column classifying each edge as OF -> Other, Other -> OF, or OF -> OF. An other_genre column isolates the non-Oceanus-Folk genre in each cross-genre influence, simplifying Tableau calculations.

Key columns: direction, influence_type, source_display_name, source_genre, source_year, target_display_name, target_genre, target_year, other_genre

oceanus_folk_year_summary.csv (40 rows × 5 columns)

A year-level summary of the Oceanus Folk genre, designed for timeline visualisations. Each row represents one release year and contains the count of OF works released, notable OF works, outgoing influence edges (OF influencing other genres), and incoming influence edges (other genres influencing OF).

Key columns: year, of_works_released, of_notable_works, of_to_other_influence_edges, other_to_of_influence_edges

artist_careers_v2.csv (1,370 rows × 16 columns)

Career profiles for all artists and musical groups with 3 or more performed or contributed works. Includes performance counts, genre breadth, career span, notable work count, and an influence reception score (performed_work_influence_received) measuring how often their works were referenced by others. Filtered to artists with meaningful careers to avoid noise from one-off contributors.

Key columns: artist_id, artist_display_name, artist_type, stage_name, num_performed_works, num_all_contributed_works, all_role_types, primary_genre, all_performance_genres, first_performance_year, last_performance_year, career_span_years, num_notable_performed_works, performed_work_influence_received, is_oceanus_folk_artist

Changes from Previous Version

The revised script differs from the original in the following ways:

Aspect Previous version Revised version
Library Python standard library only (csv, collections) pandas
Architecture One flat table per question Normalised bridge tables + summary tables
Sailor Shift’s works 38 works (direct edges only) 46 works (direct edges + group membership)
Influence direction String label "Influenced Sailor Shift" / "Sailor Shift Influenced" Standardised "Sailor -> Other" / "Other -> Sailor"
OF direction Two separate rows for each direction Single row with direction column (OF -> Other, Other -> OF, OF -> OF)
Artist careers threshold 3+ performed works only 3+ performed or 3+ contributed works
New outputs — works_master.csv, contributors_bridge.csv, influence_bridge.csv, memberof_bridge.csv, oceanus_folk_year_summary.csv
display_name Not present Derived from stage_name, falling back to name

Tools Used

Tool Purpose
Python 3 with pandas Data wrangling — reshaping graph data into normalised flat tables
Tableau Visual analytics — building interactive charts and dashboards
Gephi Network exploration — discovering patterns, communities, and key entities
